<system-reminder># System Information

Operating System: linux
Default Shell: 
Home Directory: /root
Current Working Directory: /
Current Time: Tue Jun 09 2026 05:51:50 GMT+0000 (Coordinated Universal Time)

# GIT STATUS
Current branch: 
Main branch (you will usually use this for PRs): 

Recent commits:


# TODOs
No TODOs yet, if you are working on tasks that would benefit from a todo list please use the todoWrite tool to create one.</system-reminder>

# RedwoodSDK: Multi-Room Realtime Chat ## Background A RedwoodSDK project is pre-installed at `/home/user/myapp`. Build a chat application whose room IDs are taken from the URL path and whose chat messages are kept in real-time sync per room using `useSyncedState` with a room scope. ## Requirements * Update `wrangler.jsonc` to register the `SyncedStateServer` Durable Object under the binding name `SYNCED_STATE_SERVER` (with the required SQLite migration including `"new_sqlite_classes": ["SyncedStateServer"]`). * In `src/worker.tsx`, re-export `SyncedStateServer` and register `syncedStateRoutes(() => env.SYNCED_STATE_SERVER)` inside `defineApp([...])`. * Create a client component at `src/app/components/ChatRoom.tsx` (`"use client"`) that accepts a `roomId` prop and calls `useSyncedState([], "messages", roomId)`. It must render: * A scrollable list where each message appears inside an element with attribute `data-testid="chat-message"`. * An `` for typing. * A `` button that appends the input's value to the synced array (and clears the input). * A `` button that resets messages to `[]`. * Implement `GET /chat/:roomId` as a server route that renders a page including `

Room:

` and the `ChatRoom` client component with the `roomId` parameter from the URL. ## Acceptance Criteria * Project path: /home/user/myapp * Start command: npm run dev -- --host 0.0.0.0 --port 5173 * Port: 5173 * `wrangler.jsonc` contains the `SYNCED_STATE_SERVER` durable object binding and the `SyncedStateServer` SQLite migration. * `GET /chat/general` returns HTML containing `

Room: general

` and the visible label `Send`. * `GET /chat/redwood` returns HTML containing `

Room: redwood

`. * Browser verification on http://localhost:5173/chat/general: after clicking `Clear`, typing `hello redwood`, clicking `Send`, the message text `hello redwood` appears inside a `data-testid="chat-message"` element. Then on http://localhost:5173/chat/other, after clicking `Clear`, no chat message with text `hello redwood` is visible (state is room-scoped).
I'll start by exploring the project structure to understand the codebase before making changes.
Let me read the key project files to understand the structure.

export const Document: React.FC<{ children: React.ReactNode }> = ({
  children,
}) => (
  <html lang="en">
    <head>
      <meta charSet="utf-8" />
      <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
      <title>@redwoodjs/starter-minimal</title>
      <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
      <link
        rel="preconnect"
        href="https://fonts.gstatic.com"
        crossOrigin=""
      />
      <link
        rel="stylesheet"
        href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Noto+Sans:ital,wght@0,100..900;1,100..900&family=Playfair+Display:ital,wght@0,400..900;1,400..900&display=optional"
        precedence="first"
      />
      <link rel="modulepreload" href="/src/client.tsx" />
    </head>
    <body>
      {children}
      <script>import("/src/client.tsx")</script>
    </body>
  </html>
);

part.text: Now let me look at the worker module more closely to understand the route definition API:
Now let me check how the `defineApp` and `route` work to understand how to add a route with a URL parameter:

matches.context: export declare const defineApp: <T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo<any, DefaultAppContext>, Routes extends readonly Route<T>[] = readonly Route<T>[]>(routes: Routes, options?: DefineAppOptions) => AppDefinition<Routes, T>;

export declare const realtimeRoute: (getDurableObjectNamespace: (env: Cloudflare.Env) => DurableObjectNamespace<RealtimeDurableObject>) => import("../router").RouteDefinition<"/__realtime", import("../../worker").RequestInfo<any, import("../../worker").DefaultAppContext>>;
export type Route<T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo> = RouteMiddleware<T> | RouteDefinition<string, T> | ExceptHandler<T> | readonly Route<T>[];
export type RouteDefinition<Path extends string = string, T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo> = NormalizedRouteDefinition<T> & {

type PrefixedRouteValue<Prefix extends string, Value> = Value extends RouteDefinition<infer Path, infer Req> ? RouteDefinition<JoinPaths<Prefix, Path>, Req> : Value extends ExceptHandler<any> ? Value : Value extends readonly Route<any>[] ? PrefixedRouteArray<Prefix, Value> : Value;
* Defines a route handler for a path pattern.
export declare function route<Path extends string, T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo>(path: Path, handler: RouteHandler<T> | MethodHandlers<T>): RouteDefinition<NormalizePath<Path>, T>;
* Defines a route handler for the root path "/".
export declare function index<T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo>(handler: RouteHandler<T>): RouteDefinition<"/", T>;
type RoutePaths<Value> = Value extends RouteDefinition<infer Path, any> ? Path : Value extends readonly (infer Item)[] ? RouteArrayPaths<Value> : Value extends RouteMiddleware<any> ? never : never;

export declare const syncedStateRoutes: (getNamespace: (env: Cloudflare.Env) => DurableObjectNamespace<SyncedStateServer>, options?: SyncedStateRouteOptions) => import("../runtime/lib/router.js").RouteDefinition<`/${string}`, RequestInfo<any, import("../runtime/requestInfo/types").DefaultAppContext>>[];

part.text: The issue is that the SSR bridge is trying to import the `ChatRoom.tsx` component (which has `"use client"`) during SSR, and it's hitting the pre-bundle version mismatch. This is likely because the `"use client"` directive in the component is causing issues with how the SSR bridge processes it. Let me check if there's a pattern for client components in this framework:

matches.context:
export declare const react = " # React, React Server Components, and React Server Functions Rules ## React Server Components (RSC) 1. By default, all components are server components unless explicitly marked as client components. 2. Server components are rendered on the server as HTML and streamed to the browser. 3. Server components cannot include client-side interactivity (state, effects, event handlers). 4. Server components can directly fetch data and include it in the initial payload. 5. Server components can be async and can be wrapped in Suspense boundaries. Example: ```tsx export default function MyServerComponent() { return
Hello, from the server!
; } ``` ## Client Components 1. Must be explicitly marked with the "use client" directive at the top of the file. 2. Required when the component needs: - Interactivity (click handlers, state management) - Browser APIs - Event listeners - Client-side effects - Client-side routing 3. Will be hydrated by React in the browser. Example: ```tsx "use client"; export default function MyClientComponent() { return ; } ``` ## Data Fetching in Server Components 1. Server components can directly fetch data without useEffect or other client-side data fetching methods. 2. Use Suspense boundaries to handle loading states for async server components. 3. Pass context (ctx) through props to child components that need it. Example: ```tsx export async function TodoList({ ctx }) { const todos = await db.todo.findMany({ where: { userId: ctx.user.id } }); return (
    {todos.map((todo) => (
  1. {todo.title}
    2. ))}
); } ``` ## Server Functions 1. Must be marked with the "use server" directive at the top of the file. 2. Can be imported and used in client components. 3. Execute on the server when called from client components. 4. Have access to the request context via requestInfo.ctx. 5. Can handle form submissions and other server-side operations. Example: ```tsx "use server"; import { requestInfo } from "rwsdk/worker"; export async function addTodo(formData: FormData) { const { ctx } = requestInfo; const title = formData.get("title"); await db.todo.create({ data: { title, userId: ctx.user.id } }); } ``` ## Context Usage 1. Context is available to all server components and server functions. 2. Access context via: - requestInfo in server functions: ``` import { requestInfo } from "rwsdk/worker"; const { ctx } = requestInfo ``` 3. Context is populated by middleware and interruptors and is request-scoped. ## Best Practices 1. Keep server components as the default choice unless client-side interactivity is needed. 2. Use client components only when necessary to minimize the JavaScript bundle size. 3. Leverage server components for data fetching and initial rendering. 4. Use Suspense boundaries appropriately for loading states. 5. Keep client components as small as possible, moving server-side logic to server components or server functions. 6. Always mark client components with "use client" directive. 7. Always mark server functions with "use server" directive. ";

part.text: The `"use client"` pattern is correct. The issue is likely the Vite pre-bundling cache. Let me try a different approach - let me check if there's an issue with the import path. Let me look at how the rwsdk exports its client and worker modules:

part.text:
I see - the `use-synced-state/worker` has a `workerd` condition but no `react-server` condition. But the `use-synced-state/client` doesn't have a `react-server` condition either. The problem is that the SSR bridge is trying to import the client component during SSR. Let me check if there's a `react-server` condition needed for the client import path.

The issue is that `"use client"` components need to be handled specially by the SSR bridge. The error is happening because the SSR is trying to process the `ChatRoom.tsx` component. Let me try a different approach - maybe the issue is with how the `useSyncedState` import from `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` is resolved during SSR.

Let me check if the `use-synced-state/client` has a `react-server` export:
The problem is the Vite pre-bundling cache. Let me try a more thorough cleanup and restart:

output.output:
> vite dev --host 0.0.0.0 --port 5173
[vite] An error happened during full reload
Error: There is a new version of the pre-bundle for "/home/user/myapp/node_modules/.vite/deps_ssr/react.js?v=3d5171a8", a page reload is going to ask for it.
error when starting dev server:
Error: There is a new version of the pre-bundle for "/home/user/myapp/node_modules/.vite/deps_ssr/react.js?v=3d5171a8", a page reload is going to ask for it.
    at maybeCaptureError (workers/runner-worker/index.js:50:10)

part.text:
This error is happening because the SSR bridge tries to import the `ChatRoom.tsx` file during the SSR phase, and it encounters the `"use client"` component. But this should be handled by the framework. Let me look more carefully at the error - it says it's happening at `/home/user/myapp/src/app/components/ChatRoom.tsx:6:19` which is the import line for `useSyncedState`.

Let me check if the issue is that `useSyncedState` from `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` doesn't have a `react-server` condition in its package.json exports. The SSR bridge might be trying to resolve it in the React Server environment where it shouldn't be loaded.

Let me look at how the framework handles `"use client"` components - maybe there's a special way to import client components from the server:

matches.context: console.error("Document cannot be a client component");
throw new Error(`(ssr) No module found for '${id}' in module lookup for "use client" directive`);
// Check if the final route component is a client component

throw new Error("RedwoodSDK: 'react-server' import condition needs to be used in this environment. This code should only run in React Server Components (RSC) context. Check that you're not importing server-only code in client components.");
throw new Error(`(client) No module found for '${id}' in module lookup for "use client" directive`);

part.text:
I see - the SSR bridge uses a virtual module `virtual:use-client-lookup.js` to resolve `"use client"` components. The error is happening because the pre-bundle versions are out of sync. Let me look at the actual error more carefully - it's about the pre-bundle for `react.js` having a new version. This is a Vite cache issue.

Let me try removing ALL Vite-related caches more thoroughly:

part.text:
The error is consistent - it's happening at line 6 of ChatRoom.tsx which is `import { useSyncedState } from "rwsdk/use-synced-state/client";`. The SSR bridge is trying to import this during the SSR phase, and the pre-bundle version of `react.js` has changed between the worker and SSR environments.

The issue is that the `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` doesn't have a `react-server` condition in its package.json exports. When the SSR bridge encounters the `"use client"` directive in ChatRoom.tsx, it tries to resolve the imports in that file, and `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` resolves to the default export which uses React hooks - but in the SSR environment, React might be in a different state.

Let me check if the `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` needs a `react-server` condition. Let me look at the actual `useSyncedState.js` to understand:

I see - the `useSyncedState` hook already handles the SSR case at line 20-22: if `typeof window === "undefined" && !options.hooks`, it returns `[initialValue, () => {}]`. So it should work during SSR. The issue is the Vite pre-bundling race condition.

Let me look at this from a different angle. The error says `There is a new version of the pre-bundle for "react.js"`. This is a known Vite issue where the SSR environment pre-bundles a different version than what the worker environment expects. Let me check the Vite config more carefully and see if there's a way to avoid this.

Actually, looking at the error trace more carefully:
```
at /virtual:rwsdk:ssr:rwsdk/rwsdk___ssr_bridge.js?v=4bd1d0d4:81:10
at /home/user/myapp/src/app/components/ChatRoom.tsx:6:19
```

The SSR bridge is trying to process `ChatRoom.tsx` (because it's a `"use client"` component imported from the server), and within it, it's trying to resolve the `useSyncedState` import. The `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` module imports React, and that's where the pre-bundle version mismatch occurs.

This might be a timing issue with Vite's dependency pre-bundling. Let me try running the dev server with `--force` to force re-optimization:

The `--force` flag didn't help. The issue appears to be a fundamental problem with how the SSR bridge handles the `"use client"` component that imports from `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client`. Let me look at whether there's a way to structure the import differently. 

Let me check if there's a `react-server` condition needed for the `use-synced-state/client` export. Let me look at how the `realtime/client` handles this for comparison:

I notice that `realtime/client` doesn't have a `react-server` condition either. The difference might be that `realtime/client` is not being imported by a `"use client"` component in this project.

Let me look at what happens when the SSR bridge processes the `"use client"` directive. The issue is that when the SSR bridge encounters `ChatRoom.tsx` (which has `"use client"`), it tries to resolve all its imports in the SSR context, and the `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` module imports React - but the SSR environment might have a different version of React pre-bundled.

Let me check if there's a way to make this work by looking at how other projects handle this. Let me look at the `rwsdk` e2e tests or examples:

Let me try a different approach. The issue might be that the `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` module doesn't have a `react-server` condition in its package.json exports, so when the SSR bridge processes it, it tries to load the full client module (which uses React hooks) in the SSR environment.

Let me check if there's a `react-server` condition needed:

There's no `no-react-server.js` equivalent for the `use-synced-state/client` path. The `rwsdk/client` export has a `react-server` condition pointing to `no-react-server.js`, but `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` doesn't.

This is likely a bug in the rwsdk package. However, let me try a workaround. The `useSyncedState` hook already handles SSR by checking `typeof window === "undefined"`. The issue is that the Vite SSR pre-bundling is failing because of a version mismatch.

Let me try adding the `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` to the SSR `noExternal` or try a different approach - maybe I can add it to the Vite config's `ssr.external`:

The `ssr.external` didn't help. Let me revert that and try a different approach. The issue is that the SSR bridge is trying to process the `ChatRoom.tsx` component during the initial SSR scan for `"use client"` directives. 

Let me look at the error more carefully. The stack trace shows:
1. `getWorkerEntryExportTypes` - this is trying to get the type information from the worker entry
2. `rwsdk___ssr_bridge.js` - the SSR bridge is processing the module
3. `ChatRoom.tsx:6:19` - line 6, column 19 of ChatRoom.tsx (the import statement)

This happens during the initial Vite startup, not during a request. The issue is that the SSR bridge is trying to load `ChatRoom.tsx` as part of the worker entry type analysis, and it encounters the `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` import, which triggers a pre-bundle of React in the SSR context - but there's already a pre-bundled React in the worker context with a different version.

part.text: Let me look at the ssrBridgePlugin to understand the issue better:

Now I understand the architecture better. The SSR bridge plugin intercepts module resolution in the worker environment and redirects SSR module imports to the SSR environment. The error is happening because when the worker tries to load the SSR bridge for `ChatRoom.tsx`, the SSR environment has a different version of the pre-bundled `react.js`.

This is a Vite pre-bundling race condition. The key issue is that when `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` is optimized in the SSR environment, it triggers a re-optimization of React in the SSR environment, creating a version mismatch with the worker environment.

Let me try a different approach - let me check if there's a way to avoid the issue by not having the import be directly in the worker's import chain. Let me try using a lazy/dynamic import pattern for the ChatRoom component:

```

This is happening during `getWorkerEntryExportTypes` which is called during startup. The worker is trying to analyze the types of the exported entry, and it encounters the `ChatRoom.tsx` import, which is a `"use client"` component. The SSR bridge then tries to resolve this, and it hits the pre-bundle version mismatch.

The root cause is that `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` should have a `react-server` condition in its package.json exports. Let me check if I can work around this by adding a `react-server` condition manually or by using a different approach.

The issue persists. Let me look at this from a different angle. The error says "There is a new version of the pre-bundle" - this is thrown from the worker runner when it detects that the pre-bundle hash has changed during a module load. This is a Vite internal mechanism to prevent stale modules.

Let me check the `runInRunnerObject` function in the worker runner:

matches.context: const err$2 = /* @__PURE__ */ new Error(`There is a new version of the pre-bundle for "${id}", a page reload is going to ask for it.`);

part.text:
This is `throwOutdatedRequest` - it means the pre-bundle hash changed between when the module was requested and when it was read. This happens because the SSR environment re-optimizes React when it encounters `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client`, which imports React.

The key insight is: the worker environment already has React pre-bundled. When the SSR bridge tries to load `ChatRoom.tsx` (a `"use client"` component), it goes through the SSR environment. The SSR environment then encounters `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` which imports React. This triggers a re-optimization of React in the SSR environment, which changes the hash. Meanwhile, the worker environment still has the old hash reference.

Let me look at whether adding `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` to the `optimizeDeps.include` or `optimizeDeps.exclude` in the Vite config would help. Or maybe I should add it to the SSR external modules.

Actually, looking at the `ssrBridgePlugin.mjs` code more carefully, I notice that it has logic to handle the `externalModulesSet`. Let me check if I can add `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client` to the external modules for the SSR environment.

But wait - the `externalModulesSet` is defined in the rwsdk package, not in user config. Let me try a different approach.
